old_claim.note: Notarial Records provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim_absent: No direct edge remains because civic_record_offices is anchored to 1086 Norman administrative records, while the notarial_records node is now anchored to 1155 Genoese notarial registers.

source_shape.source_locator: The National Archives Domesday source supports the 1086 administrative-record anchor for civic_record_offices, not dependence on later Genoese notarial registers.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The civic record office anchor is an 1086 administrative survey/record system rather than a twelfth-century notarial register.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: Keeping the edge would create an anachronistic dependency from 1086 civic records to a notarial-register node dated 1155.

ontology_before: Civic record offices depended directly on notarial_records, partly via a later Lucca notarial archive source that did not match the 1086 Domesday anchor.
ontology_after: Civic record offices remain scoped to municipal/administrative archives without requiring the later Genoese notarial-register tradition.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed: civic_record_offices no longer has a direct notarial_records prerequisite.
would_reject_if: Civic record offices were redated to late medieval city archives that explicitly incorporated notarial registers.
A source showed the 1086 Domesday administrative-record anchor depended on notarial registers.
The notarial_records node were split and one child represented earlier administrative legal records compatible with 1086.
short_reason: The 1086 civic-record scope should not depend on a 1155 Genoese notarial-register node.
